customs-health-declaration.description: China customs and health declarations explained: why the COVID health card was scrapped in Nov 2023, when a health declaration is still required, the customs goods declaration, duty-free and cash limits, and how all this differs from the immigration Arrival Card.
customs-health-declaration.title: China Customs and Health Declaration Explained: What You Actually Need in 2026
keyTakeaways: The COVID-era Customs Entry/Exit Health Declaration Card was scrapped for all travelers on 1 November 2023. You do not fill one out anymore in normal circumstances.
A health declaration is now only required if you have symptoms or a contagious disease, or temporarily during an outbreak when authorities reinstate it.
The customs goods/baggage declaration is a separate, still-active process. Use the green channel if you have nothing to declare and the red channel if you exceed allowances or carry restricted items.

Duty-free allowances for arriving travelers include roughly 400 cigarettes and 1,500 ml of alcohol (12%+), plus reasonable personal-use items. Cash over RMB 20,000 or foreign currency over the equivalent of US$5,000 must be declared.
None of these customs steps is the same as the China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC), an immigration entry declaration launched by the National Immigration Administration on 20 November 2025.
The official Arrival Card is free on NIA channels, must be filed within 72 hours before arrival, and is required for most foreign nationals, including 30-day visa-free and 240-hour transit visitors.

sections.heading: Three Different Things Travelers Keep Confusing
body: If you are planning a trip to mainland China, you have probably run into a confusing tangle of forms, codes, and declarations online. Much of the advice you will find is out of date, because the rules changed significantly between 2023 and 2025.

The simplest way to understand it is to separate three completely different things. First, there is the customs side: declaring goods, cash, and (in rare cases) your health to China Customs. Second, there is the immigration side: declaring who you are and why you are entering, which is now done through the China Digital Arrival Card. Third, there is the old COVID-era health declaration, which most travelers no longer need at all.

This guide walks through each one in plain English so you know exactly what still applies in 2026 and what you can safely ignore. iVisa Portal is an independent travel-document help site, not a government agency, so we will also point you to the official, free channels where relevant.

sections.heading: The Old COVID Health Declaration (And Why It's Gone)
body: During the pandemic, every traveler entering or leaving China had to complete the Customs Entry/Exit Health Declaration Card. You filled out a survey about symptoms and recent health, received a QR code, and showed it to customs officers at the border. It was a real bottleneck, and it is the source of most of the outdated 'health code' advice still floating around.

That requirement was scrapped for all travelers on 1 November 2023 by China's General Administration of Customs. It was the last remaining COVID-19 entry requirement to be lifted. In normal circumstances today, you do not fill out any health declaration card, and there is no health QR code to generate before a routine trip.

If a website tells you that you must complete a health declaration QR code to enter China for tourism in 2026, it is describing a system that no longer exists. Treat that as a red flag for outdated or unreliable information.

sections.heading: When a Health Declaration Is Still Required
body: Scrapping the routine card did not eliminate health rules entirely. A health declaration is still required in two situations, and it is worth knowing them so you are not caught off guard.

First, if you have symptoms of, or have been diagnosed with, an infectious disease, you are obligated to declare your health situation to Customs on your own initiative. Relevant symptoms include fever, cough, difficulty breathing, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, or unexplained bleeding under the skin. This is a self-declaration duty, not a form everyone fills in.

Second, during a public-health outbreak, authorities can temporarily reinstate a health declaration requirement at the border. This is a contingency measure, so always check current conditions close to your departure date if a notable outbreak is in the news.
bullets: Routine trip, feeling well: no health declaration needed.
Symptoms or a contagious illness: you must declare to Customs voluntarily.
Active outbreak period: a temporary declaration may be reinstated, so verify before you fly.

sections.heading: The Customs Goods Declaration: Still Very Much Active
body: Separate from anything health-related, China still operates a standard customs goods and baggage declaration system. This is the familiar red-channel / green-channel setup you see at airports worldwide.

If you are carrying only personal items within the allowances and nothing prohibited or restricted, you take the green channel ('nothing to declare'). If you exceed the duty-free limits, carry restricted goods, or are bringing in cash above the reporting thresholds, you take the red channel and complete a Customs declaration. Choosing green when you should have chosen red can lead to penalties, so when in doubt, declare.

Commonly declared or restricted categories include large quantities of goods that look commercial rather than personal, certain food and agricultural products, medicines, and high-value electronics intended to stay in China. This goods declaration has nothing to do with your immigration entry form.

sections.heading: Duty-Free Allowances and Cash Thresholds
body: For arriving travelers, China's duty-free allowances for personal use are generous but finite. As a guide, you can typically bring in around 400 cigarettes (or equivalent cigars or tobacco) and up to 1,500 ml of alcoholic drinks at 12% alcohol or above, along with a reasonable quantity of personal items. Goods beyond the duty-free limit may be released only after you pay the applicable customs duty.

Cash has its own rules and trips up many travelers. You must declare if you are carrying more than RMB 20,000 in Chinese currency, or foreign currency worth more than the equivalent of US$5,000. If you bring in foreign currency above that US$5,000 equivalent and intend to take it back out, you complete a declaration form in duplicate and keep the endorsed copy for your departure.

Allowances and figures can be adjusted by the authorities, so treat these as planning guidance rather than guarantees, and confirm current limits with official China Customs information before you travel with anything close to the thresholds.
bullets: Tobacco: about 400 cigarettes, or 100 cigars, or 500 g of tobacco, duty-free.
Alcohol: up to roughly 1,500 ml of drinks at 12%+ alcohol, duty-free.
Cash: declare RMB over 20,000 or foreign currency over the US$5,000 equivalent.
Over the limit: excess personal-use goods are released after you pay duty.

sections.heading: How This Differs From the Immigration Arrival Card (CDAC)
body: Here is the part most affected by recent change. Entirely separate from customs is the immigration entry declaration, now handled by the China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC), often just called the Arrival Card (ru jing ka). China's National Immigration Administration launched it on 20 November 2025, replacing the old paper arrival card.

The Arrival Card is an immigration document. You provide your passport details, flight or transport information, where you are staying in China, and your purpose of visit. Filing produces a QR code that is scanned at the border. Importantly, it is not a visa and does not by itself grant you entry; it simply pre-registers your arrival information so the immigration check is faster.

The official card is free on NIA channels, including the web form at s.nia.gov.cn/ArrivalCardFillingPC, the NIA 12367 app, and the WeChat and Alipay mini-programs. It must be filed within 72 hours (three days) before you arrive. Be cautious of third-party sites charging high fees while implying they are official; you can always use the free government channels directly.

sections.heading: Who Needs the Arrival Card, and Practical Tips
body: The Arrival Card is required for foreign nationals entering mainland China by air, land, or sea. Crucially, this includes travelers using the 30-day visa-free policy and those on the 240-hour (10-day) visa-free transit, which covers 55-plus countries through designated ports and requires a confirmed onward ticket to a third country. Visa-free does not mean declaration-free.

There are seven NIA-exempt categories that do not need to file: foreign permanent-resident-card holders; Hong Kong and Macao permit holders; group-visa entrants; passengers in airside transit under 24 hours; same-ship cruise passengers; e-channel users; and transport crew. If you do not fit one of these, plan to file.

A few practical habits keep entry smooth. File the Arrival Card within the 72-hour window once your flight and hotel are confirmed, and save or screenshot the QR code in case of patchy airport Wi-Fi. Keep your customs situation honest and simple: if you are over an allowance or carrying significant cash, take the red channel. And remember the mental model: customs handles your goods, cash, and any health issues; immigration handles you and your Arrival Card. They are not the same line, the same form, or the same QR code.
bullets: File the free Arrival Card on official NIA channels within 72 hours of arrival.
Visa-free and 240-hour transit travelers still need it unless in an exempt category.
Screenshot your Arrival Card QR code as a backup before you land.
At customs, declare cash over the thresholds and use the red channel when over allowances.

240-hour-visa-free-transit.title: China's 240-Hour (10-Day) Visa-Free Transit: A Practical 2026 Guide
240-hour-visa-free-transit.description: A plain-English guide to China's 240-hour visa-free transit: who qualifies, the 55+ eligible countries, ports and regions, the onward-ticket rule, and the Arrival Card.

sections.heading: What the 240-Hour Visa-Free Transit Actually Is
body: China's 240-hour visa-free transit lets eligible travelers stop over in mainland China for up to 10 full days without applying for a visa in advance, as long as they are passing through on the way to a third country or region. In practice, it is one of the most generous transit arrangements in the world: you arrive, get stamped in at the border, and you are free to explore for the better part of two weeks before flying or sailing onward.

The key word is transit. This is not a tourist visa and it is not a residence permit. It exists so that people travelling between two other places can break their journey in China. Your itinerary has to look like A to China to C, where A and C are different countries or regions from each other and from mainland China. If you simply want to fly round-trip from home to China and back, the transit policy does not apply, and you would need an ordinary visa instead.

The 240-hour clock starts at 00:00 on the day after you arrive, not the minute you land. That quirk works in your favour, effectively giving you a little extra time on arrival day. Even so, treat 10 days as a firm ceiling and build in a buffer before your onward departure.

sections.heading: Who Qualifies: The 55+ Eligible Countries
body: The policy is open to ordinary passport holders from a fixed list of 55+ countries. The list covers most of Europe, several countries in the Americas, key Asia-Pacific nations, and a handful of others. It is the same core list used for the older 144-hour transit policy, simply extended to the longer 240-hour window.

Eligible nationalities include the United States, Canada, the United Kingdom, Ireland, almost all EU and Schengen-area countries, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, Malaysia, Russia, Brazil, Argentina, Mexico, Qatar, the UAE, and Indonesia, among others. Indonesia was the most recent addition, bringing the total to 55.

Because the list is updated periodically as China adds countries, always confirm your specific nationality against the current official list before you book. If your passport is not on it, you are not eligible for transit and will need a standard Chinese visa.
bullets: Common eligible regions: most of Europe and the Schengen area, North America, and major Asia-Pacific nations
Recently added: Indonesia (55th country)
If your nationality is not on the list, the transit policy does not apply to you

sections.heading: Eligible Ports and Regions
body: You cannot use this policy at just any border crossing. Entry and exit must happen through a designated open port, and you must stay within the region tied to that port. As of late 2025, China expanded the program to roughly 65 ports across 24 provinces, autonomous regions, and municipalities, including major hubs in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Chengdu, Xian, and many others.

A notable November 2025 expansion added ports in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao area, such as Guangzhou, Zhuhai's Hengqin, the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macao Bridge, and the West Kowloon Station of the Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong Express Rail Link. This made cross-boundary trips in the Greater Bay Area much easier for transit travelers.

Crucially, within the 10 days you are generally expected to remain in the province or designated region you entered through, though several clustered regions allow movement across multiple provinces. Check the specific stay area for your port of entry before you travel, because rules differ from one region to another.

sections.heading: The Onward-Ticket Rule
body: This is the requirement that trips up the most people, so get it right. To qualify, you must hold a confirmed onward ticket, with a fixed date and seat, to a third country or region within 240 hours of your arrival. Airline staff at your departure airport and immigration officers on arrival will both want to see it.

Third country means somewhere other than where you came from. Flying London to Shanghai to Tokyo qualifies; London to Shanghai to London does not, because your origin and final destination are the same. Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan count as separate regions for this purpose, so a route such as Bangkok to Beijing to Hong Kong is valid.

Book a real, confirmed onward ticket rather than relying on a flexible or open-ended booking, and keep both a printout and a digital copy. If your onward leg falls outside the 240-hour window, you will be refused boarding or entry, so leave a comfortable margin.

sections.heading: What You Can and Cannot Do
body: Within the 10 days, the permitted activities are tourism, business meetings, visiting friends or family, and short cultural or social exchanges. For ordinary leisure travel and most business trips, that covers everything you are likely to need.

What you cannot do is work, study in a formal program, undertake journalism or news reporting, or any other activity that normally requires a specific visa category. These require prior approval and the appropriate visa before you arrive, regardless of how short your stay is.

If your plans change and you need to stay longer than 240 hours, or travel outside your permitted region, contact the local exit-entry administration of the public security bureau to apply for an appropriate stay permit. Overstaying or wandering outside your designated area can lead to fines and complications on future trips.

sections.heading: How the Arrival Card Fits In
body: Separate from the visa-free policy itself, nearly every foreign traveler entering mainland China now needs to complete the China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC), also called the Arrival Card. China's National Immigration Administration launched it on 20 November 2025, replacing the old paper arrival card. It applies to entries by air, land, and sea, and that explicitly includes 240-hour visa-free transit travelers as well as 30-day visa-free visitors.

The Arrival Card is an immigration entry declaration. You provide your passport details, your flight or transport information, your accommodation in China, and the purpose of your visit. Submitting it generates a QR code that is scanned at the border. It is important to understand what it is not: it is not a visa, it does not grant entry, and it does not replace the onward-ticket or eligibility rules of the transit policy. It simply digitizes the declaration you used to fill in on paper.

File it within 72 hours (3 days) before you arrive. The card is free on the official NIA channels, including the website at s.nia.gov.cn/ArrivalCardFillingPC, the NIA 12367 app, and the WeChat and Alipay mini-programs. Any site charging a mandatory fee just to submit the card is not an official channel. A few categories are exempt from the card, such as holders of permanent-resident cards, Hong Kong and Macao permit holders, group-visa entrants, airside transit under 24 hours, same-ship cruise passengers, e-channel users, and transport crew.

One more point that often causes confusion: the COVID-era Customs Health Declaration Card was scrapped for all travelers on 1 November 2023. A health declaration is now only needed if you have symptoms or a contagious disease, or during a declared outbreak. A separate customs declaration for goods and baggage still exists and is unrelated to the Arrival Card.

sections.heading: Practical Tips Before You Fly
body: A little preparation makes the difference between a smooth stopover and an awkward conversation at the check-in desk. The points below are the ones travelers most often overlook.
Treat the onward ticket and the Arrival Card as two separate, equally important boxes to tick. Eligibility gets you in the door; the Arrival Card gets you through the border smoothly.
bullets: Confirm your nationality is on the current 55+ list before booking anything
Book a confirmed onward ticket to a genuine third country or region within the 10-day window, with a fixed date and seat
Verify your specific port of entry is one of the designated ports and learn its permitted stay region
Tell the airline at check-in that you intend to use 240-hour visa-free transit, so they apply the right rule
File the free Arrival Card within 72 hours of arrival via an official NIA channel, and save the QR code offline
Carry printed and digital copies of your onward ticket, hotel booking, and passport
Build in a buffer day before your onward flight so a delay never pushes you past 240 hours

sections.heading: A Note on Sources and This Guide
body: This guide is published by iVisa Portal, an independent travel-document assistance resource. We are not affiliated with the Chinese government or the National Immigration Administration, and we cannot issue visas, approvals, or Arrival Cards on your behalf.

Immigration rules change, and ports, eligible countries, and regional stay rules are updated from time to time. For the binding, up-to-date details, confirm against official Chinese government and NIA sources or your nearest Chinese embassy or consulate before you travel. The official Arrival Card is free on NIA channels, and you never have to pay a mandatory fee to submit it.

keyTakeaways: China's 240-hour transit lets eligible travelers stay up to 10 days visa-free, but only while transiting from one country or region to a different third country or region.
Citizens of 55+ countries qualify, including the US, UK, most of Europe, Australia, Japan, South Korea, and recently Indonesia; always confirm your nationality on the current list.
You must enter and exit through one of roughly 65 designated ports across 24 provinces and regions, and generally stay within the region tied to your entry port.
A confirmed onward ticket to a different third country or region, with a fixed date and seat within 240 hours, is mandatory and checked at check-in and the border.
Tourism, business, and visiting family are allowed; work, study, and journalism are not and need a proper visa arranged in advance.

Almost all entrants, including transit travelers, must file the free China Digital Arrival Card within 72 hours of arrival via official NIA channels; it is a border declaration, not a visa, and does not grant entry.

china-entry-requirements.title: China Entry Requirements 2026: Visa, Visa-Free Rules, and the Mandatory Digital Arrival Card Explained
china-entry-requirements.description: A clear 2026 guide to entering China: visa vs visa-free, the mandatory China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC), passport rules, what to pack, and the airport entry process.
keyTakeaways: Almost every foreign traveler now needs the free China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC), filed online within 72 hours before arrival — including 30-day visa-free and 240-hour transit visitors.
The CDAC is an immigration declaration that produces a QR code; it is NOT a visa and does not by itself grant you entry to China.
Check whether you need a visa or qualify for a visa-free scheme, and make sure your passport meets validity rules before you book.
The COVID-era health declaration card was scrapped on 1 November 2023 — you only declare health now if you have symptoms or during an outbreak.
Seven categories (including China green-card holders and short airside transit passengers) are exempt from the arrival card.

sections.heading: Start Here: The Three Things That Decide Your Entry
body: Planning a trip to mainland China in 2026 feels more complicated than it actually is. Almost everyone's path to the border comes down to three separate questions, and it helps to keep them apart in your head.

First: do you need a visa, or do you qualify to enter visa-free? Second: does your passport meet China's validity rules? Third — and this is the new one for 2026 — have you filed the China Digital Arrival Card before you fly? Each is handled in a different place, by a different system, and getting one right does not cover the others.

The big change travelers keep tripping over is that last point. Since 20 November 2025, China's National Immigration Administration (NIA) requires a digital arrival declaration that replaces the old paper card you used to scribble on the plane. This guide walks through all three steps in plain English, in the order you'll actually deal with them.

A quick note on who we are: iVisa Portal is an independent travel-document help site, not a government agency. We explain the rules and the process; we don't issue visas or arrival cards, and the official arrival card is free on China's own government channels.

sections.heading: Visa vs Visa-Free: Which Path Is Yours?
body: Your nationality, your reason for visiting, and how long you'll stay determine whether you need a visa. Many travelers do still need to apply for one in advance through a Chinese embassy, consulate, or visa center — that's the traditional route for longer stays, work, study, or nationalities not covered by an exemption.
But China has expanded visa-free travel significantly. Two schemes matter most for tourists and business visitors in 2026.

The 30-day unilateral visa-free policy lets ordinary-passport holders from a list of eligible countries enter for tourism, business, family visits, or transit for up to 30 days without applying for anything in advance. The 240-hour (10-day) visa-free transit policy covers travelers from 55+ countries passing through China en route to a third country — you enter at a designated port, stay up to 240 hours, and must hold a confirmed onward ticket to a third country or region.

Two things people miss: visa-free is not the same as paperwork-free, and the transit scheme genuinely requires that onward third-country ticket. If you're flying in and out of the same country, the transit policy doesn't apply to you.
bullets: Need longer stays, work, or study? You almost certainly need a visa applied for in advance.
Short tourism or business trip and an eligible nationality? Check the 30-day visa-free list.
Just passing through to another country? The 240-hour transit policy may fit — but only with an onward third-country ticket.
Visa-free travelers still must file the arrival card (covered below).

sections.heading: Passport Validity: Don't Get Caught at Check-In
body: Airlines check passports at the gate, and a passport that's too close to expiry can end your trip before it starts. As a rule of thumb, plan for at least six months of remaining validity for visa-based travel, and make sure you have blank pages for stamps.

For the 240-hour visa-free transit scheme specifically, the published requirement is an ordinary passport with at least three months of validity. Different schemes can carry different thresholds, so the safest approach is simple: renew early if you're anywhere near the line.

Check the expiry date today, not the night before you fly. Passport renewals can take weeks, and a near-expiry passport is one of the most common, most avoidable reasons travelers get turned away at check-in.

sections.heading: The Mandatory Digital Arrival Card (CDAC): What It Is and Isn't
body: This is the part most likely to catch returning visitors off guard. The China Digital Arrival Card (CDAC) — officially the Arrival Card, 入境卡 — launched on 20 November 2025 and replaced the paper arrival card that used to be handed out on flights.

It is an immigration entry declaration. You provide your passport details, flight or transport information, where you'll stay in China, and the purpose of your visit. The system then generates a QR code that border officers scan when you arrive.

Here's the critical distinction: the CDAC is NOT a visa, and it does not by itself grant you entry. It's a declaration, not permission. You still need the right visa or a valid visa-free basis to be admitted — the arrival card simply replaces the old paper form in that process.

It applies broadly. Foreign nationals entering mainland China by air, land, or sea generally must complete it — and that explicitly includes travelers using the 30-day visa-free policy and the 240-hour visa-free transit scheme. Many people assume visa-free means card-free. It doesn't.

sections.heading: How and When to File the CDAC (and Who's Exempt)
body: The official arrival card is free. You file it on the NIA's own channels: the website at s.nia.gov.cn/ArrivalCardFillingPC, the NIA 12367 app, or the official WeChat and Alipay mini-programs. Be wary of third-party sites charging a fee for the card itself — the government doesn't charge for it.

Timing matters: you must submit it within 72 hours (3 days) before your arrival. Filing too early won't work, so add it to your pre-trip checklist for the few days before departure. If you don't manage it beforehand, ports generally provide electronic devices to complete it on arrival — but doing it in advance keeps your border crossing quick.

Seven categories are exempt from the card. These include holders of a Foreign Permanent Resident ID Card (China green card); non-Chinese nationals holding a valid Mainland Travel Permit for Hong Kong or Macao; travelers entering on a group/collective visa or group visa-free arrangement; airside transit passengers staying under 24 hours without clearing immigration; cruise passengers arriving and departing on the same ship; e-channel (automated gate) users; and transport crew.
bullets: File on official NIA channels only — the card itself is free.
Submit within the 72 hours before you arrive.
Keep the QR code accessible (screenshot it) for the border check.
Visa-free and transit travelers are included — exemptions are narrow and specific.

sections.heading: Health and Customs Declarations: What Changed
body: Good news for anyone who remembers the pandemic-era forms: the Customs Entry/Exit Health Declaration Card was scrapped for all travelers on 1 November 2023. You no longer routinely fill one out.

A health declaration is now required only in specific situations — if you have symptoms of or have been diagnosed with a contagious disease (think fever, cough, difficulty breathing, vomiting, diarrhea, rash), or during a declared outbreak when authorities reinstate checks. If you're unwell on arrival, you're expected to declare it voluntarily to Customs.

Separately, a customs goods and baggage declaration still exists. This is the standard process for declaring items you're bringing in — currency over limits, restricted goods, commercial quantities, and so on. It's distinct from both the immigration arrival card and the old health form, so don't confuse the three.

sections.heading: Step by Step: From Booking to the Arrivals Hall
body: Putting it all together, here's the realistic sequence for a smooth entry in 2026. Tackling these in order means no last-minute scrambles at the gate or the border.

Following this checklist, most travelers clear immigration in minutes. The arrival card and customs declaration live in different systems from your visa, so confirm each one rather than assuming a single step covers everything.
bullets: Before booking: confirm whether you need a visa or qualify for 30-day visa-free or 240-hour transit; check your passport validity and renew if it's close to the line.
If required: apply for your visa, or (for transit) book the onward third-country ticket your scheme requires.
Within 72 hours before arrival: file the free China Digital Arrival Card on official NIA channels and save the QR code.
At the airport on arrival: have your passport, visa or visa-free documents, and CDAC QR code ready for immigration; officers scan the code and check your basis for entry.
After immigration: complete the customs goods/baggage declaration if you have anything to declare; only fill a health declaration if you have symptoms or one is required during an outbreak.
Keep digital and paper backups of key documents in case of patchy connectivity or app issues at the port.
